messages.content:
The error in the code is because the table "Computers" is being referenced in the Filling procedure before it has been created. You need to create the "Computers" table before inserting data into it.

Also, make sure that the table names are correct and exist in the database. If there are any other dependencies between the tables, make sure to handle them accordingly in the code.
Как вставить в другой пакет процедуры другого пакета

Чтобы использовать процедуры из одного пакета в другом пакете, вы можете использовать декларацию `IS` из первого пакета в декларации пакета второго пакета.

Вот пример того, как можно вставить процедуры из одного пакета в другой:

Предположим, у вас есть пакет `CreatingDataBase` с процедурами, которые вы хотите использовать в другом пакете.

```sql
-- Пакет CreatingDataBase
CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE CreatingDataBase IS
    PROCEDURE Filling;
    PROCEDURE Deleting;
END CreatingDataBase;
/
```

Теперь допустим, у вас есть другой пакет `AnotherPackage`, в котором вы хотите использовать процедуры из пакета `CreatingDataBase`.

```sql
-- Пакет AnotherPackage
CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE AnotherPackage IS
    PROCEDURE UseFillingFromCreatingDB;
END AnotherPackage;
/
```

Чтобы использовать процедуру `Filling` из пакета `CreatingDataBase` в пакете `AnotherPackage`, вы можете сделать следующее:

```sql
-- Пакет AnotherPackage
CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE BODY AnotherPackage IS
    PROCEDURE UseFillingFromCreatingDB IS
    BEGIN
        CreatingDataBase.Filling; -- Вызов процедуры из другого пакета
    END UseFillingFromCreatingDB;
END AnotherPackage;
/
```

Теперь при вызове процедуры `UseFillingFromCreatingDB` из пакета `AnotherPackage`, она будет использовать процедуру `Filling` из пакета `CreatingDataBase`.
как на английском планшетный компьютер
Tablet computer

To add constraints to the views, you can specify the columns and conditions in the SELECT statement of the VIEW. Here is an example of adding constraints to the views:

```sql
-- Drop the existing view Firms_view
DROP VIEW Firms_view;

-- Create the Firms_view with constraints
CREATE VIEW Firms_view AS
    SELECT firm_id, firm_name
    FROM Firms
    WHERE firm_id < 5; -- Adding a constraint to show only firms with firm_id less than 5

-- Drop the existing view Computers_view
DROP VIEW Computers_view;

-- Create the Computers_view with constraints
CREATE VIEW Computers_view AS
    SELECT computer_id, frequency, type_id, firm_id
    FROM Computers
    WHERE frequency > 2000; -- Adding a constraint to show only computers with frequency greater than 2000
```

In this example, I added constraints to restrict the columns shown in the views `Firms_view` and `Computers_view` based on certain conditions. You can modify the conditions in the WHERE clause as needed to apply different constraints.
GRANT CompFirmPack.ChangeFirmName ON USER	Error at line 1/7: ORA-00990: missing or invalid privilege

The error message "ORA-00990: missing or invalid privilege" indicates that the privilege specified in the GRANT statement is not recognized or does not exist in Oracle.

In Oracle, you can only grant privileges that are valid and supported by the database. The privilege specified in the GRANT statement must be one of the valid system or object privileges.

If you want to grant the privilege to execute a specific procedure (`ChangeFirmName`) in a package (`CompFirmPack`), you can use the EXECUTE privilege along with the name of the package and procedure.

Here is an example of how you can grant the EXECUTE privilege for a specific procedure in a package to a user:

```sql
GRANT EXECUTE ON CompFirmPack TO USER;
```

Replace `USER` with the name of the user to whom you want to grant the privilege.

Make sure that `CompFirmPack` is the correct package name and `ChangeFirmName` is the correct procedure name within that package.
GRANT EXECUTE ON CompFirmPack TO USER	Error at line 1/34: ORA-00987: missing or invalid username(s)

The error message "ORA-00987: missing or invalid username(s)" indicates that the specified username is not valid or missing in the GRANT statement. 

Make sure to replace `USER` with the actual username to whom you want to grant the EXECUTE privilege on the package `CompFirmPack`.

Here is an example of the GRANT statement with a valid username:

```sql
GRANT EXECUTE ON CompFirmPack TO <username>;
```

Replace `<username>` with the actual username of the user to whom you want to grant the EXECUTE privilege on the package `CompFirmPack`.

After updating the username in the GRANT statement, you should be able to grant the EXECUTE privilege successfully.
В какой таблице посмотреть список всех пользователей

To see a list of all users in an Oracle database, you can query the `DBA_USERS` view. This view contains information about all users in the database. 

Here is an example query to retrieve the list of all users:

```sql
SELECT username
FROM dba_users;
```

When you run this query, it will return the usernames of all users in the database along with other information such as their account status, default tablespace, and more. 

Please note that you may need appropriate permissions to access the `DBA_USERS` view. If you do not have access to the `DBA_USERS` view, you can also query the `ALL_USERS` view to see users that you have access to view.

GRANT EXECUTE ON ChangeDataBasePack TO 'WKSP_PINPEX'

Error at line 1/40: ORA-00987: missing or invalid username(s)

The error message "ORA-00987: missing or invalid username(s)" indicates that the username should not be enclosed in single quotes in the GRANT statement. When specifying a username in the GRANT statement, you should not use quotes around the username.

Here is the correct syntax for granting the EXECUTE privilege on a package to a user:

```sql
GRANT EXECUTE ON ChangeDataBasePack TO WKSP_PINPEX;
```

Please make sure to replace `ChangeDataBasePack` with the actual package name and `WKSP_PINPEX` with the correct username to whom you want to grant the EXECUTE privilege. After correcting the username syntax, the GRANT statement should work without any errors.

To add cascading delete constraints to two tables in Oracle, you can use the `ON DELETE CASCADE` option when defining the foreign key constraints. This will ensure that when a record in the parent table is deleted, all related records in the child table are automatically deleted as well.

Here is an example of how to add cascading delete constraints to the `Firms` and `Computers` tables:

```sql
-- Drop the existing foreign key constraint on the Computers table
ALTER TABLE Computers
DROP CONSTRAINT fk_firms;

-- Add a new foreign key constraint with cascading delete
ALTER TABLE Computers
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_firms
FOREIGN KEY (firm_id) REFERENCES Firms(firm_id)
ON DELETE CASCADE;
```

In the above script, the existing foreign key constraint `fk_firms` on the `Computers` table is dropped first, and then a new foreign key constraint with cascading delete is added. This will ensure that when a record in the `Firms` table is deleted, all related records in the `Computers` table with the corresponding `firm_id` will be deleted automatically.

After running the above script, the cascading delete constraints will be in place for the `Firms` and `Computers` tables in your database.
